summary: McKenzie announces the beta launch of his Bingo Card Creator website, built with minimal tools (Notepad and a free design template) in about four hours. He shares his detailed pre-launch checklist covering website pages, payment processing setup, data files, and platform support. The post captures the scrappy, ship-fast ethos of a solo developer getting a product to market. He candidly admits to forgetting about needing a software license until hours before launch.
key_points: A functional product website can be built with free tools and minimal time investment — perfection is not required for launch
The 'beta' label serves as permission to ship something half-finished and iterate
Payment processing logistics (Payloadz, eSellerate) are real friction points for solo software businesses
Strategic decisions about free vs. paid content tiers matter even for simple products
Last-minute oversights like forgetting a software license are normal parts of the solo founder experience
money_quotes: If you're wondering how good of a website you can get accomplished with Notepad, a free design from www.oswd.org, and about four hours of tinkering… well, here's your chance to find out!
Half is good enough to slap the word "beta" on and shove it out the door.
I believe it to be of shippable quality, at least on PCs.
Unlikely I'll be getting many registrations on launch day.
It just hit me about three hours ago that I should probably include a license. I'm mildly panic stricken as to the prospect.
key_insight: Launching a software product as a solo developer means shipping something imperfect quickly and solving problems as they arise, rather than waiting for everything to be polished.
